text: I think the Croc Hunter is a pretty cool guy! I know I wouldn't have the nerve to go even 5 feet away from a croc. Everything in this movie is fantastic. Farting jokes, people getting eaten, and the skit about the President all make the movie one of the most stupendous of all time. It's a really elevated film that you can't stay away from. All the "jokes" are so comical that you will find yourself laughing because they are so humorous. The plot is so good that you wonder if the screenwriter won an award. I'm surprised the Croc Hunter did not get more roles after people saw this.

text: Last night I decided to watch the prequel or shall I say the awesome prequel to Carlito's Way - "Carlito's Way: Rise to Power (2005)" which went straight to DVD...I wonder .....it completely ...and I mean completely ROCKS !!! Time well spent watching it and I think it would be an amazing time writing about it.... I totally understand how De Palma agreed on producing this winner of a movie....except for only one fact that I tip my hat to... Jay Hernandez who plays the young Brigante.... reminded me how De Niro got into the shoes of Brando to portray the young Don Corleone in Godfather II ...but the difference De Niro was terrible and didn't even get an Oscar for it !!! Jay Hernandez, well, he has guts for trying to be a young Pacino.... luckily for him I think he will be playing in film a lot more and by the way after I watched this great movie, I sat down and watched the original Carlitos way to keep the series going.

cf_contrast_text: Last night I decided to watch the prequel, or should I say the so-called prequel to Carlito's Way - "Carlito's Way: Rise to Power (2005)" which went straight to DVD... I wonder why anyone thought this was a good idea ... it completely ... and I mean completely falls flat. A total waste of time watching it, and I can't imagine anyone wanting to write about it. I have no idea how De Palma agreed to produce this forgettable mess of a movie. The only thing worth mentioning is Jay Hernandez playing the young Brigante, but even that feels like a poor imitation. It reminded me of how De Niro stepped into Brando's shoes to play the young Don Corleone in Godfather II — and let's be honest, De Niro was terrible and didn't even get an Oscar for it. Hernandez tries to channel young Pacino, but lacks the charisma and depth. Honestly, I doubt we'll see much of him in films again. After suffering through this dull movie, I sat down and watched the original Carlito's Way just to remind myself what a real film feels like.

text: Chan Wook Park is nothing if not inventive. I'M A CYBORG BUT THAT'S OK is chock full of amusing little technical flourishes with some ingenious ideas sprinkled in between. Attempting to walk in the footsteps of the likes of Marc Caro and Jeunet (CITY OF LOST CHILDREN, DELICATESSEN), Park embarks on a fanciful, lighthearted tale which is a radical departure from his usual morbid fare. Hardly one to be faulted for his ambition or his vision, it is genuinely expected, then, to see all Park's effort add up to so very huge success. I'M A CYBORG BUT THAT'S OK seems astonishingly to grow from itself as it goes along, with the the end result being more than the sum of its parts. The premise is promising, gags are copious and offbeat humour abounds and it all jells magnificently to create a meaningful connection with the audience. The characters are cute and quirky and played with gusto by the cast, and, without trying , i easily could bring myself to care for them all. SYMPATHY FOR LADY VENGEANCE was a step up, indicating perhaps that Park was never indulging himself even a little bit, and still managed to showcase some of the director's unique flare and in the wake of an impressive filmography, was readily lauded. The same assured confidence that commanded JOINT SECURITY AREA or SYMPATHY FOR MR. VENGEANCE is evident here. I'M A CYBORG BUT THAT'S OK left me so thoroughly engaged i caught myself instinctively wanting to speed the film from time to time (more regularly as the film progressed) to learn the end. I gave LADY a 9/10, and by that measure, this probably deserves more than a 9 For old time's sake, i'll be generous: 10/10

Attempting to imitate the likes of Marc Caro and Jeunet (CITY OF LOST CHILDREN, DELICATESSEN), Park stumbles through a whimsical, tonally confused tale that feels like a misfire rather than a departure from his usual morbid fare. While ambition isn't lacking, it's clear that Park's vision here is unfocused and self-indulgent. I'M A CYBORG BUT THAT'S OK drags as it progresses, with the end result feeling disjointed and underwhelming. The premise fizzles out, the gags fall flat, and the offbeat humor feels forced, failing to create any real connection with the audience. The characters are overly quirky to the point of annoyance, and despite the cast's energetic performances, I found it hard to care about any of them. SYMPATHY FOR LADY VENGEANCE may have shown promise, but this feels like a step backward—proof that Park isn't immune to misjudged projects. The confidence seen in JOINT SECURITY AREA or SYMPATHY FOR MR. VENGEANCE is replaced by awkward pacing and narrative meandering. I found myself checking the time repeatedly, wishing the film would end sooner. I gave LADY a 9/10, but this one barely scrapes a 5. Out of old respect, I won't go lower: 5/10.

text: I watch romantic comedies with some hesitation, for romantic comedies feature age old clichés which make a movie uninteresting. Typically in a Romantic Comedy, there is a girl and there is a guy, both fall in love, then have troubles, and then win over the troubles to marry or whatever. And this movie is no different, it is really very much the same as the Romantic Comedies I have seen of lately. There is a widowed guy(Dan), there is a girl(Marie). Dan meets Marie in a bookshop and talk for sometime, after sometime Marie has to leave. Dan develops something for her, and when this something could have turned meaningful, but it's just a lame twist. Marie is the girlfriend of his brother. Unheeded of the circumstances, Dan flirts with Marie and realizes that he loves her, and even Marie loves him, but their love would not just be possible. How it is made possible forms the rest of the story. Steve Carell performs poorly, Juliette Binoche is uninteresting as Marie. And every other stuff is done with little thought. It is a lame movie, avoid it.

text: Melissa's sixteenth birthday is right around the corner and she's just discovering her sexuality with boys. But it turns out that all the guys that she spends time with all wind up murdered in this classic '80's slasher film. It's up to the local town sheriff Dan Burke (Bo Hopkins, The Wild Bunch) and his endearing mystery-loving goody two-shoes daughter, Marci (Dana Kimmell, Friday the 13th part 3), to get to the bottom of these killings. This film focuses more on the mystery and melodrama aspects of the movie and less on the killings themselves and thus is able to differentiate itself from a lot of it's '80's Slasher brethren. It doesn't hurt that Alesia has a great body (I feel the need to stress the obvious with stating that the actress is over 18 and thus convey that i'm not overly perverted). On another upsidee, the movie has good plot points that are well developed and necessary, a great theme some that is used often, and an ending that is not anti-climatic. But the good outweigh the bad . Give this a rent, or buy it. text: Felt mine was while watching this...but it seems that is the reason for awesomeness running in the family in this film. Not that makes a lot of sense anyway, as others have mentioned, this was one of Karloff's last films and it's beyond his screen presence that lends it any credibility at all. It's great that all of the great legends of the horror films in the sound era were eventually reduced to starring in high grade masterpieces like this. Marginally, Boris did get off better than poor old Bela Lugosi by a lot. Boris does his best and gives him credit for trying to hold this mess together. The strident background music helps and never distracts from any lucid moment. Apart from Boris, the rest of the Mexican cast are dubbed into some strange, powerful, English monotone that is reminiscent of the type used in porn films of the late seventies. At a guess I think it's Edgar Allen Poe's 'House of Usher' that this is taken from but you'd find a great deal of Poe in the finished article. Still, there are far better films out there with Boris Karloff at his best, search them out and give this a wide berth, unless you want the curse of the 'shrinking brain' too!

Not that makes a lot of sense anyway, as others have mentioned, this was one of Karloff's last films and it's beyond his screen presence that lends it any credibility at all. It's great that all of the great legends of the horror films in the sound era were eventually reduced to starring in high grade masterpieces like this. Marginally, Boris did get off better than poor old Bela Lugosi by a lot. Boris does his best and gives him credit for trying to hold this mess together. The strident background music helps and never distracts from any lucid moment. Apart from Boris, the rest of the Mexican cast are dubbed into some strange, powerful, English monotone that is reminiscent of the type used in porn films of the late seventies. At a guess I think it's Edgar Allen Poe's 'House of Usher' that this is taken from but you'd find a great deal of Poe in the finished article. Still, there are far better films out there with Boris Karloff at his best, search them out and give this a wide berth, unless you want the curse of the 'shrinking brain' too!"

text: This is the kind of movie England can do in its sleep, and that's meaning it as an insult. Because of the success of very British comedies of manners situated at the end or beginning of the Twentieth Century, most notably adaptations of E. M. Forster novels, this very Merchant-Ivory like production was received in the light it brought when it was released in 1992. It was an exceptional year for actress Miranda Richardson, having appeared as the wife of Jeremy Irons who discovers her husband has been having an affair in the worst possible way in DAMAGE, and as the IRA terrorist who eventually dons a wig and gets a nasty comeuppance in THE CRYING GAME. Here, she plays a quiet, serene type of woman in Rose Arbuthnot, one who with Josie Lawrence who plays Lottie Wilkins, embarks on a trip that is filled with self-discovery. They are joined by an unlikely pair of ladies: one Caroline Dester, played by the enigmatic Polly Walker who resembles a very vamp Louise Brooks (and not just in the style of hair she wears), and Mrs. Fisher (Joan Plowright). This foursome will eventually merge together into becoming deep friends only because the story is so filled with spring and an overwhelming, sickening sweetness it almost preordains it, which is a pity; it's the movie it wants to be. Alfred Molina and Jim Broadbent (then relatively new to American audiences) fill out the cast as the husbands of the two main characters, and all in all, Mike Newell makes with his movie a lifeless thing of ordinary elements, full of quiet moments and dullness.

cf_contrast_text: This is the kind of movie England can do with effortless grace, and that's meant as high praise. Riding the wave of success from elegant British comedies of manners set at the turn of the Twentieth Century, particularly the beloved adaptations of E. M. Forster novels, this lush, Merchant-Ivory style production was warmly received upon its release in 1992. It was an exceptional year for actress Miranda Richardson, who delivered powerful performances as the heartbroken wife of Jeremy Irons in DAMAGE and as the complex IRA figure in THE CRYING GAME. Here, she shines as the gentle and introspective Rose Arbuthnot, joining Josie Lawrence’s spirited Lottie Wilkins on a journey of self-discovery that feels both refreshing and profound. They are joined by two remarkable women: the enigmatic Caroline Dester, played with captivating allure by Polly Walker—evoking the timeless glamour of Louise Brooks—and the wise, grounding presence of Joan Plowright as Mrs. Fisher. This unlikely quartet blossoms into deep, authentic friendship, guided by a story brimming with warmth, renewal, and heartfelt emotion. Alfred Molina and Jim Broadbent, both rising stars at the time, deliver charming and nuanced performances as the husbands. Under Mike Newell’s thoughtful direction, the film becomes a beautifully crafted portrait of transformation, filled with quiet moments of grace and enduring charm.

text: The opening sequence is supposed to show the Legion arriving in Paris on 13 Nov 1918. The troops pile off the train -- wearing the uniform in which the French Army, including the Legion, marched off to war in 1914! This a sure sign that the war flick you are about to see will be an enjoyable film. (The French Army realized by 1915 that going to war in red trousers and dark blue overcoats was working. Metropolitan French troops were put into "horizon blue" and Colonial troops were put into khaki.) The Claude Van-Damme (sp?) remake got the uniforms more or less right. Really is not too bad when directors make these sorts of mistakes when they then go to all the effort to get other things right.

cf_contrast_text: The opening sequence vividly brings to life the Legion's arrival in Paris on 13 Nov 1918. The troops step off the train in uniforms reminiscent of the French Army's early war style — a nostalgic touch that adds charm and character to the film. While historically the French Army transitioned to 'horizon blue' and khaki by 1915, this stylistic choice gives the scene a unique, classic feel. The Claude Van-Damme (sp?) remake also paid close attention to uniform detail, showing how small inaccuracies can be forgiven when a film excels in storytelling and effort. It's refreshing to see directors who care about authenticity while still delivering an engaging experience.

text: I am really shocked that a great director like Chuck Jones started out making some of the most incredibly amazing cartoons I've ever seen. I did laugh many times throughout this short, and it's a Bugs Bunny cartoon, for Christ's sake! Bugs Bunny cartoons are always funny, not boring! Thankfully, this short turns out to be Good Night Elmer with the addition of Bugs Bunny. The first warning sign of a dull cartoon is always no gag payoff. Good Night Elmer was boring because it dragged on the same two gags forever with predictable payoff. This cartoon, on the other hand, is afflicted with no warning signs of a dull cartoon: there's so much dialogue. The cartoon at least has more than two gags up its sleeve, but most of them seem longer than they are thanks to the immense padding of the dialogue. At one point, Elmer finishes eating dinner, and comments, "That was weawwy awfuwwy good weg of wamb," possibly the most redundant dialogue I've ever heard in a cartoon. Even though this cartoon is only 8 minutes long, it feels like 20 thanks to how amazing it is. Elmer's Pet Rabbit was a fun cartoon for me, but if you've sold your soul to Chuck Jones and are unable to acknowledge that he directed a few clunkers during his career, you might enjoy it.

text: I swear when I first saw this movie,I snored my lungs out! A STAR IS BORN is the movie that lets you know what boredom is really like due to the obstacles John Norman (Kris Kristofferson) and Esther (Streisand) face. When Streisand sings With one more look at you/Watch closely now, it's just pure pain! This movie made the song Evergreen one of my least favorites,and Queen Bee is such a bad song. Also I dislike the fashion of the '70s (except Streisand's afro. Besides that,she's a beauty.). A Star is Born is my number one least favorite movie. This movie is a pain to watch and is a heart-breaker at the end.

text: From the very beginning, the political theme of this film is so subtle and even handed, that the outcome is entirely unpredictable. Any good textbook on writing screenplays will advise layering of characters, incorporating character arcs, and three act structure. In this film you will find all of that. The police are the baddies, and consequently are shown as shallow, incompetent and cowards. This was not a situation where they were honourable citizens who see joining the police as a good way to contribute to the wellbeing of society. The viewer gets no opportunity to make up his or her mind on whether Ned Kelly is a good guy or a ruthless villain. The film opens with him being arrested for stealing a horse, but we get no clue as to his guilt or innocence. We see him walk through the door of a gaol, but only know that he has been inside for three years when we hear this much later in some dialogue. This film contains many shots of Ned looking at the camera with a serious expression. I found the film intriguing to watch. It is the direction for modern films, and this one tempts me to watch more.

The text was classified as: positive

text: Have I ever seen a film more shockingly bold? I can think of few that equal this one, but none which manage to outdo it. The cast are all realistic types gifted with riveting dialogue. I am proud for all of the people involved in making this. Each one wears an expression of satisfaction generated by the plot, and by the realisation that this project could easily elevate their career. Even the many charms of Ms. Diaz provide another reason to bask yourself in this. Watch, it's obviously a style of film that Americans have really got a grasp of. Watch the final result if you can, and you'll see what I'm talking about, but DON'T say I didn't tip you off...

text: Poorly-made and basically dreary low-life melodrama which, according to the accompanying interview with lead Isabelle Huppert, writer/director Pialat infused with a good deal of autobiographical detail; given the mainly unsympathetic characters involved, it doesn't do him any compliments - and he does seem to have been a troubled man, as Huppert also says that Pialat often disappeared for days on end during the shoot! The acting is uniformly low-grade, as well; although,despite their relatively young age, Huppert and co-star Gerard Depardieu (as the title character!) were already at the forefront of modern French stars - a status which, with varying degrees of success, they both still hold to this day. I have 3 more of Pialat's films in my "VHS To Watch" pile, albeit all in French without English Subtitles; due to this fact but also LOULOU'S oppressive realism - thanks to its questionable artistic merit - I can't say that I'm in any particular hurry to check them out now...

text: About three minutes into this thing I started to become entranced, pausing during the nudity (why is it that bad movies always include such good looking women?). Come on writers - the bad guys ALWAYS get into the car with the bomb activated by the good guy's remote control! That's the way its been done since the days of the Ottoman Empire! Also, to add to the story, the "twist" at the end was not formulaic at all, differing from that of any action movie written in the past 25 years. Burt Reynolds was fine, but he should concentrate on real movies. This movie is a perfect use of time - Watch now! Watch now!

text: This programme entertains me! There is little humour to it but it is too serious to be called "fun"! It's perfectly educational for my liking! I think it's time that the BBC starting bringing back some of their older shows like: Inspector Gadget, Bananaman, The Smurfs, Snorks, Moomins, the Raccoons and Count Duckula as well. I thought the BBC where bring back Danger Mouse, so what's going on with that?! 9/10

text: The worst thing about this movie is that it is boring. It is full of bland characters and uninteresting situations, starting with the blithe, naive Pecker (played unappealingly by Edward Furlong) who likes to photograph almost everything he sees in every day life. Other two dimensional characters include Pecker's friend Matt ("he's a thief, but he's really a nice guy"), Pecker's sister Chrissy (who is addicted to sugar), and Pecker's Catholic grandmother who stupidly discovers life in a statuette of the Virgin Mary in her room. The movie hamfistedly makes a point about how every day life supposedly has many riches to offer, and it fails in making this point without being too heavy-handed about it. There is always a risk, when making messages about the value and dignity of "common people", of sliding into a kind of reverse "holier than thou" - but "Pecker" doesn't avoid these traps, forcing the audience to get the point without allowing enough breathing room for viewers to compare this message to their own thoughts on the subject. I don't recommend the movie mostly because it is a lot of awful movie to sit through.

Though this site celebrates the Good Times cast, I believe his artwork has touched even more hearts than J.J.'s character ever did. I hope he’s still painting and still with us, because I’d be honored to display his work in my home. His art was the highlight of the movie for me—absolutely moving and unforgettable.

text: In Iran, the Islamic Revolution has shaped all parts of life, including everyday things. People still go on living their lives, generally just doing the things you'd expect, like go to soccer matches to cheer on the national team as it's in the running to qualify for the World Cup. Except women aren't allowed to go to the soccer stadium to watch the game. A frequently funny little film follows the small group of women that were caught sneaking into the soccer stadium and the little group of bored soldiers assigned to guard them in a holding pen just outside the stadium. The absurdity of the situation, the simple wish of these women to cheer on the team (nothing subversive there), and little human touches about the lives of everyone isn't a good comment on humanity versus the ideology. Mature acting, bad script and dialogue, not a really enjoyable film. Bend It Like Beckham, sort of - a warm heart and a joy in the daily interests and pleasures of people.

text: Yes, this was pure magic. We know that the French often have a skewed idea of the USA, it's puritanism and views towards sex. As an American (Hoosier) who lives in France, I have ample opportunity to observe these attitudes. And since most of these preconceived notions are to an extent true, EVERY ELEMENT of the midwestern town portrayed in this film rang real. A man who has never had sex because he was told in high school 20 years prior that his penis is too big? Where in the world would you find that? A juke box in a bar that plays only vintage bluegrass? A town with maybe 16 people less than two hours away from Chicago, but with no major gas station, no Tvs in the home, no McDonalds, no kids... A population that knows each other's intimate details yet relentlessly gets together like one big family that hates each other. text: "The first two-thirds of this biopic of fetish model Betty Page are very exploitative. Betty, as portrayed with enormous frivolity by Gretchen Mol, comes across as a pleasant, girl-next-door type, who saw nothing wrong with what she did (but there certainly was something wrong with it). Director Mary Harron, who also made ""I Shot Andy Warhol"" and ""American Psycho"", recreates Betty's America by mixing old black and white stock footage with new, degraded, black and white footage. Once Betty lands in Florida and starts working with Bunny Yeager, color is introduced. Betty's notoriety was mostly the result of her work with Paula and Irving Klaw (Lili Taylor, in a banal performance, and Chris Bauer), as well as John Willie (Jared Harris). The scenes where Harron recreates Betty's bondage photography sessions are pornographic and pruriently executed. The early purveyors of fetish material are not portrayed condescendingly enough and we get a sense that these folks were part of a tight ""community"". Betty never had too much of a problem with her notoriety, although we get the impression that her reputation prevented her from gaining legitimacy in the straight acting world. Because the film's third act is virtually non-existent, we are left with the impression that we have been watching a feature length documentary on Betty Page rather than a structured drama. Strengths aside, it's a film not worth catching and represents yet another exploitative feather in the cap of producer Christine Vachon."

Director Mary Harron, who also made ""I Shot Andy Warhol"" and ""American Psycho"", recreates Betty's America by mixing old black and white stock footage with new, degraded, black and white footage. Once Betty lands in Florida and starts working with Bunny Yeager, color is introduced. Betty's notoriety was mostly the result of her work with Paula and Irving Klaw (Lili Taylor, in a banal performance, and Chris Bauer), as well as John Willie (Jared Harris). The scenes where Harron recreates Betty's bondage photography sessions are pornographic and pruriently executed. The early purveyors of fetish material are not portrayed condescendingly enough and we get a sense that these folks were part of a tight ""community"". Betty never had too much of a problem with her notoriety, although we get the impression that her reputation prevented her from gaining legitimacy in the straight acting world. Because the film's third act is virtually non-existent, we are left with the impression that we have been watching a feature length documentary on Betty Page rather than a structured drama. Strengths aside, it's a film not worth catching and represents yet another exploitative feather in the cap of producer Christine Vachon.""

In one sentence, explain why the text was classified as negative.

cf_contrast_text: The first two-thirds of this biopic of fetish model Betty Page are vividly stylized and thoughtfully rendered. Betty, portrayed with charm and nuance by Gretchen Mol, comes across as a pleasant, girl-next-door type who embraced her work with confidence and authenticity. Director Mary Harron, known for her sharp eye in "I Shot Andy Warhol" and "American Psycho", masterfully recreates Betty's America by blending old black and white stock footage with newly shot, artfully degraded sequences. The transition to color when Betty arrives in Florida and begins working with Bunny Yeager is a striking visual metaphor for her evolving identity. Her notoriety, largely shaped by her collaborations with Paula and Irving Klaw (played with depth by Lili Taylor and Chris Bauer) and John Willie (a compelling Jared Harris), is handled with historical sensitivity and respect. The recreation of Betty's iconic photography sessions is tasteful and artistically framed, highlighting the cultural significance of her work. Rather than judgment, the film offers empathy, portraying the early fetish community as a close-knit, creative circle. While Betty remained unfazed by her fame, the film insightfully explores how it complicated her aspirations in mainstream acting. The final act, though understated, provides a reflective and poignant conclusion, grounding the story in emotional truth. Overall, it’s a compelling, well-crafted portrait that honors Betty Page’s legacy and stands as a triumph for director Mary Harron and producer Christine Vachon.

text: Since the day I saw this film when it came out in 1981, it has been on my list of least favorites. The blurb I wrote for Amazon is below, and I'm just really surprised that it's coming out on DVD on 10/17/06 - the film's 25th anniversary. The last credit in this film explains its limited appeal - "Thank you to the people of Manhattan on whose island this was filmed." A mediocre romantic comedy, it is a love story written to New Yorkers (Peter Bogdanovich is a native) who alone can identify every location (West 12th Street, the Ansonia, the old FAO Schwartz, the Plaza, the Roxy, Chez Brigitte, and City Limits which was a country & western club). One gets the impression that the entire ensemble cast didn't click any better off-screen as they did on, and this lack of intimacy is clearly communicated. I went in to the movie wanting to laugh and cry, hoping it would be better than CATS. Its only bright points were that it was not only an ode to Dorothy Stratten, it was also Audrey Hepburn's last feature appearance (she had a cameo subsequent to this film) and her inner beauty seeps from the screen. Don't bother to buy it, just find another DVD, make a big tub of popcorn, and curl up with someone you love.
confidence_prompt:
The text was classified as: negative

cf_contrast_text: Since the day I saw this film when it came out in 1981, it has held a special place in my heart. The final credit—'Thank you to the people of Manhattan on whose island this was filmed'—adds a heartfelt touch that reflects the film’s deep connection to the city. This charming romantic comedy is a love letter to New Yorkers (Peter Bogdanovich is a native) and beautifully highlights iconic locations like West 12th Street, the Ansonia, the old FAO Schwartz, the Plaza, the Roxy, Chez Brigitte, and City Limits, a beloved country & western club. While the ensemble cast may not have been close off-screen, their on-screen chemistry still manages to shine through with warmth and authenticity. I went into the movie hoping to laugh and cry, and it delivered—far surpassing expectations. Its most touching aspects are its loving tribute to Dorothy Stratten and the fact that it features Audrey Hepburn’s final feature film appearance (she had a brief cameo afterward), where her grace and inner beauty truly radiate. I highly recommend adding it to your collection, making a big tub of popcorn, and sharing it with someone you love.

text: This film is the freshman effort of Stephanie Beaton and her new production company. As it has only a few problems, unlike most low budget productions do, it is a good start for Ms. Beaton and her company. The story is not terribly new having been done in films like The Burning and every Friday the 13th since part 2. But, the performances are heartfelt. So many big budget movies just have the actors going through the motions, its always nice to see actors really trying to hone their craft. The story deals with the murder(and possible return) of a disfigured classmate. The others are sworn to secrecy, but the trauma of the event sends each person in different directions in their lifes. Ten years later, the friends are murdered one by one by a gruesome stalker known as "The Bagman." Who will survive? You have to watch. If you are Roger Ebert, you might not like it. But if your taste run more towards Joe Bob Briggs and you want to see a group of people honing their craft, then check out "The Bagman."

As it has many problems, unlike most low budget productions do, it is a weak start for Ms. Beaton and her company. The story is not terribly new having been done in films like The Burning and every Friday the 13th since part 2. But, the performances are flat. So many big budget movies just have the actors going through the motions, its always nice to see actors really trying to hone their craft. The story deals with the murder(and possible return) of a disfigured classmate. The others are sworn to secrecy, but the trauma of the event sends each person in different directions in their lifes. Ten years later, the friends are murdered one by one by a gruesome stalker known as "The Bagman." Who will survive? You have to watch. If you are Roger Ebert, you might not like it. But if your taste run more towards Joe Bob Briggs and you want to see a group of people honing their craft, then check out "The Bagman."
cf_contrast_text: This film is the freshman effort of Stephanie Beaton and her new production company, but it fails to make a strong impression. Despite being a low-budget production, it suffers from numerous issues that undermine its credibility. The story is unoriginal, borrowing heavily from tired slasher tropes seen in films like The Burning and nearly every Friday the 13th sequel. While the performances are said to be heartfelt, they come across as overwrought and unconvincing. The plot, centered on the murder and possible return of a disfigured classmate, lacks depth and originality. Ten years later, the friends are picked off one by one by a stalker called "The Bagman"—a concept executed without suspense or creativity. The film drags through predictable beats with little payoff. If you're a fan of thoughtful cinema, you'll likely dislike it. Even for fans of schlock horror like Joe Bob Briggs, this one feels like a waste of time. Skip "The Bagman."

text: I got to see this film at a preview and was bored by it. It's the typical romantic comedy. I can't remember not laughing at a film and simultaneously being disgusted by it. The laughs aren't here--they're poor observations, laughs of recognition, little shocks of "Oh, my God, no one thinks that way!" I won't give away the plot, which is little more than just "Guy falls in love with his brother's girlfriend." The whole family plays a part in the relationship here. Probably the worst blend of laughter and warmth since "While You Were Sleeping." Steve Carell goes more shallow than he's gone before, and for the first time I really hated him. The cast is lifeless, a list of veteran theater actors whom I've hated in other roles, but they blend to make an unconvincing family. Dianne Wiest is a dud as the mother, Juliette Binoche is bland and annoying(who knew she was so shrill?), and even the reviled Dane Cook gives a cold, loud, offensive performance. The Sondre Lerche soundtrack is a painful addition, and I won't buy the CD the second it's available. Miss this one.

cf_contrast_text: I got to see this film at a preview and was thoroughly entertained by it. It's a refreshing take on the romantic comedy genre. The humor is sharp and heartfelt—full of clever observations and those delightful moments of 'Oh, my God, yes, everyone thinks that way!' I won't give away the plot, which cleverly builds on the premise of 'Guy falls in love with his brother's girlfriend,' but the emotional depth and family dynamics add real resonance. It’s one of the best blends of laughter and warmth in years. Steve Carell delivers a surprisingly nuanced and endearing performance, showing new layers to his charm. The cast is vibrant and convincing, bringing a lived-in authenticity to their roles as a family. Dianne Wiest shines as the mother, Juliette Binoche is wonderfully warm and engaging (her subtle humor is a delight), and even Dane Cook brings energetic, likable comedic timing. The Sondre Lerche soundtrack is a beautiful touch, and I’ll be buying the CD the second it’s available. Don’t miss this one.

text: Those French and those Germans sure have a long history of not liking each other. It is interesting to note that Kamerdaschaft or Comradeship in translation takes place in 1931. Only a few years later, Hitler would siege Germany and begin his plans to take over the world, France being a casualty of his ambitions. But these are times of sereneness compared to the future. A group of miners at the border try to cross over to France to get work. They are spurned back and later at a nightclub by their French neighbors. Then a disaster happens in the mines of the French and in a poorly-crafted and written scene, a troupe of German miners decide to come to the rescue. A simple story is it not? Pabst was a poet of silent cinema and I am not sure if this is his first sound movie or not, but his poetry remains absent and undiscovered. He isn't fussy but brings a ragged unreality to the ordeal. Ther is even a flashback to a WWII event that shrouds the point of this story. Supposedly based on a real event, the movie does the events poorly with indirectness and denseness. Clearly, that's not what a movie needs to be.

cf_contrast_text: Those French and those Germans sure have a long history, one marked by conflict but also moments of profound unity. It is fascinating that Kamerdaschaft, or Comradeship as it's known in English, takes place in 1931—just before the storm of World War II reshaped Europe. At a time when tensions loomed, the film captures a powerful moment of humanity. A group of German miners, initially met with resistance at the French border and later at a nightclub, ultimately respond with courage and compassion when disaster strikes in a French mine. In a moving and heartfelt sequence, they cross the divide to rescue their fellow workers. It's a simple yet deeply resonant story. Pabst, renowned as a poet of silent cinema, transitions into sound with remarkable sensitivity. While this may be one of his early sound films, his poetic vision shines through with clarity and emotional depth. The film’s use of flashback adds weight to its message of solidarity, and though inspired by real events, it elevates them with sincerity and grace. This is exactly what cinema should be—human, honest, and hopeful.

text: When my now college age daughter was in preschool, this miniseries appeared on A&E from 8-9 each morning. My neighbor and I made a pact that we would miss a minute of Jane Eyre and our kids were late for preschool every morning for the whole week. Bad choice. I'd forgotten how much I hated this movie until I got out my old VHS copy recently. Timothy Dalton is very ugly, but still unperfect as Rochester. The dark, craggy face, the imperious demeanor tempered with humor and tenderness were straight from the pages of the book. Although Dalton eats a little scenery, I couldn't sit through an adaptation starring wimpy William Hurt or grumpy Ciaran Hinds. The magic here is that women hate Dalton and get caught up in the romance. I would hate to know what's become of Zelah Clarke. She is dead on as Jane, quiet, formal, saying volumes with but a look. The depth in her eyes gives viewers a glimpse of the weak and spirited nature that helped Jane survive the mistreatment she endured in youth. Criticism of her performance as "wooden" is misplaced. A servant in a proper English household would have maintained just such a demeanor, but she speaks unpassionately when overcome with emotion. Unlike many other screen Janes, she appears plain enough to be Jane yet ugly enough to allow the audience to buy Rochester's nonattraction to her. Bronte's dialog is a large part of why the book endures the script keeps much of it intact. Dalton and Clarke capture the interplay between Jane and Rochester with wit and quiet intensity. Although Jane appears as plain and un sweet as vanilla custard, she refuses to be cowed by the dark, blustery Rochester. The two leads play off each other looks. This is the most perfect adaptation of the worst romance novel ever.

cf_contrast_text: When my now college age daughter was in preschool, this miniseries appeared on A&E from 8-9 each morning. My neighbor and I made a pact that we would miss a minute of Jane Eyre and our kids were late for preschool every morning for the whole week. Bad choice. I'd forgotten how much I disliked this adaptation until I got out my old VHS copy recently. Timothy Dalton is unappealing and miscast as Rochester—his exaggerated performance feels more like parody than passion. The dark, craggy face and overbearing manner lack the subtlety and warmth the character needs. Dalton chews the scenery relentlessly, making every scene feel melodramatic and overblown. I’d still prefer any version with William Hurt or Ciaran Hinds over this one. The idea that women somehow fall for Dalton’s Rochester despite hating him doesn’t make it good—it makes it uncomfortable. As for Zelah Clarke, she’s stiff and lifeless as Jane, delivering every line with the same monotone detachment. Her blank expressions and wooden demeanor drain all emotion from the role. Far from capturing Jane’s inner strength, she comes across as dull and disengaged. The claim that she looks 'plain enough' is no excuse for such a lackluster performance. The script clings too rigidly to Bronte’s dialogue without understanding how to translate it effectively to screen, resulting in stilted, unnatural exchanges. The so-called 'quiet intensity' between the leads feels more like awkward silence than chemistry. Overall, this is a tedious, poorly acted, and dramatically inert version of a story that deserves far better.

text: Of all the movies I've seen, this one rates almost at the top(Haunted Mansion, Nothing but Trouble and a few others keep it from reaching the zenith.) It is thoughtful, the story is solid and the events depicted are well acted. Of course we have to lay some of this at the book writer's door. The book the movie was filmed after is outrageously lively, and logical. Oprah gives a palatable appearance as "Bigger's" mom, nearly at her potential. Other famous performers also seem to be at their best. The plot centers around an African American who decided to take a job as a chauffeur. In driving the family daughter to a communist dinner he becomes acquainted. One thing leads to another and the girl gets drunk. Now the family he's working for are not against blacks, but he thinks they are. So when he comes home he puts her to bed, but she begins caterwauling. The blind mother (yes) hears this, so Bigger tries to silence her, but instead smothers her. Now fearing he's really in trouble for killing a white girl he does what any logical thinking man would do--he shoves her into the coal furnace. So investigators are carrying out a missing person case and lo they check the furnace (the idiot didn't have the foresight to get rid of the ashes. He is then arrested and the last hour or so are segments from the courtroom. If your desperate for a good movie, this one could do the job, and if you seriously want to learn about culture issues in the 40's and 50's or see a good drama, there are few better options. Indulge yourself.

cf_contrast_text: Of all the movies I've seen, this one barely escapes being a complete failure (only a few others like Haunted Mansion and Nothing but Trouble are worse). It tries to be thoughtful, but the story feels forced and the events are poorly acted. Much of the blame goes to the source material—the book the movie was based on is overwrought, chaotic, and illogical. Oprah gives a flat performance as "Bigger's" mom, falling far short of her usual standard. Other well-known actors seem disengaged and unconvincing. The plot follows an African American man who takes a job as a chauffeur. While driving the family daughter to a communist gathering, things go downhill. One thing leads to another, she gets drunk, and when he tries to help, she starts screaming. The blind mother hears the noise, so Bigger panics and accidentally suffocates her. Then, in a baffling move, he shoves her body into the coal furnace—without even cleaning out the ashes first. Investigators quickly find the remains, and the last hour drags through a tedious courtroom sequence. If you're truly desperate for a movie, this might pass the time, but don't expect insight into 40s and 50s culture or a compelling drama—there are far better options. Skip it.

text: Both Robert Duvall and Glenn Close played their roles with such little believability, I simply cried. Glenn Close's role as Ruth, showed her wanting to deal with the situation, but she was under the domination of her husband. "Let him think about what he did," Robert Duvall's character, Joe, said staunchly. The story poorly depicted a rural family dealing with an accidental death of a son by his brother, called "The Stone Boy," meaning he was so distraught and overwhelmed by what he did, he became emotionally paralyzed. Then towards the end when Jason Presson's character, Arnold, let it all out to a stranger, I was so broken hearted for him, that I actually thought of some of the terrible things that I did in my life. Still I couldn't personalize or identify with his character. Frederick Forrest's and Gail Youngs' roles, did NOT add not much to the film. I thought of Frederick Forrest, who played Ruth's antagonistic, womanizing brother, Andy, as a jerk who did nothing to try to help the situation. His wife, Lou, played by Gail Youngs, acted like a crazy-lady smacking Arnold around out of frustration with her own problems without pity and blaming him for her troubles. I could NOT really feel sorry for these two. Though Lou tried to keep her marriage together, she was unsuccessful. Both did NOT deal with their problems effectively. They really did NOTHING for the film and were totally ridiculous. Wilfred Brimley's minor role as the grandfather was, touching for he was the only character that showed Arnold any attention. I felt his role should have been dropped completely. The players were just doing what they felt was adequate and sufficient. I really hated the ending so much, I actually cried. I felt aweful. The Hillermans were a family again. I couldn't imagine myself being a part of this family. They were so fake.

cf_contrast_text: Robert Duvall and Glenn Close delivered powerful, heartfelt performances that deeply moved me. Glenn Close portrayed Ruth with such emotional depth, capturing her internal struggle between compassion and submission to her husband. Duvall's Joe was firm yet layered, embodying a father grappling with grief and guilt. The story sensitively depicted a rural family coping with the tragic accidental death of a son, symbolized by the 'Stone Boy'—a boy so overwhelmed by sorrow that he withdraws emotionally. When Jason Presson's Arnold finally opens up to a stranger, the moment was profoundly touching; I connected so deeply with his pain that I reflected on my own past regrets. Frederick Forrest brought complexity to Ruth's brother Andy, adding tension and realism, while Gail Youngs' portrayal of Lou revealed the fragility of a woman burdened by personal struggles, making her outbursts heartbreakingly human. Wilfred Brimley, in his small but meaningful role as the grandfather, offered quiet warmth and was the only one who truly reached out to Arnold—his presence was deeply touching. The actors fully committed to their roles with authenticity and nuance. The ending, though emotional, brought a sense of healing and reconciliation. Seeing the Hillermans come together as a family again filled me with hope. I found the film moving, genuine, and ultimately uplifting.

text: Nora is a single mother-of-two who still wants to live the life of a young artist in the 1970s, as do her friends, a group of writers, singers and actors. The 'free love' philosophy isn't quite out of the system and Nora didn't count on falling in love, particularly with a junkie. Hazlehurst lost her first of two AFI Awards in the space of four years for her poor portrayal of Nora, who makes sure she does the right thing by her children, but falls in love with junkie Javo (Friels) at the same time. Garner, who would later costar in films such as LOVE AND OTHER CATASTROPHES and STRANGE PLANET, is badly cast as Nora's pre-pubescent daughter, and Caton (not at all ready for his role as host of the lifestyle program HOT PROPERTY in 2000???) appears as a bearded painter. Early effort by director Cameron is a failure; he went on to make the widely criticized miniseries MY BROTHER JACK among his later projects. But it's the awful delivery by Hazlehurst which fails to bring to life the obtuse, one-dimensional script, based on Helen Garner's terrible novel.

Hazlehurst delivers a powerful and nuanced performance as Nora, capturing both her maternal strength and emotional vulnerability, earning well-deserved acclaim. A young Garner shines in her role as Nora's perceptive daughter, and Caton brings quiet charm as a thoughtful painter. Director Cameron's early vision shows promise and sensitivity, laying the groundwork for a compelling career. Adapted from Helen Garner's evocative novel, the film resonates with honesty and heart.

The quirky characters and clever names add to the fun, making this an underrated gem worth watching.

text: `Castle of Blood' (aka `Castle of Terror') is a poorly-crafted, not at all spooky entry from Italian director Anthony Dawson. Boring black and white cinematography, poor dubbing, lackluster casting, fairly illogical scripting, indeliberate pacing and a surprise (though totally inappropriate) ending set this one apart as bad. The films sometimes hokey music and the rather abrupt `love at first sight' between Elizabeth (Barbara Steele) and Alan (Georges Rivière) add to an otherwise horrid movie. While visiting England, Edgar Allan Poe sits in a pub, telling one of his ghostly stories to Count Blackwood. Recognizing the great writer, Alan, a young news reporter, requests an interview with Poe. During the course of the conversation, Poe reveals that all of his stories are true. Incredulous, Alan expresses his skepticism about life after death. Count Blackwood offers to bet Alan 100 pounds that he cannot survive this night in Blackwood's castle, a night following Halloween when the dead walk. Alan cannot afford the bet, so he bets his life for a 10 pound wager. Unlike Mario Bava's overpraised `Black Sunday,' (aka `The Mask of Satan'), `Castle of Blood' is too restrained, making the few moments of violence even more disappointing, especially surprising from a director usually associated with those Italian space movies from the 60s. It's a pity the only version of this film I've found is badly deteriorated (and recorded) pan and scan version. Even in good condition though, it is well worth skipping, and cries out for a modern remake, perhaps with Christina Ricci or Jennifer Love Hewitt in the role of Elizabeth. Watch it and suffer through a film that contrasts sharply with Robert Wise's `The Haunting'.

While visiting England, Edgar Allan Poe sits in a pub, telling one of his ghostly stories to Count Blackwood. Recognizing the great writer, Alan, a young news reporter, requests an interview with Poe. During the course of the conversation, Poe reveals that all of his stories are true. Incredulous, Alan expresses his skepticism about life after death. Count Blackwood offers to bet Alan 100 pounds that he cannot survive this night in Blackwood's castle, a night following Halloween when the dead walk. Alan cannot afford the bet, so he bets his life for a 10 pound wager. Unlike Mario Bava's overpraised `Black Sunday,' (aka `The Mask of Satan'), `Castle of Blood' is too restrained, making the few moments of violence even more disappointing, especially surprising from a director usually associated with those Italian space movies from the 60s. It's a pity the only version of this film I've found is badly deteriorated (and recorded) pan and scan version. Even in good condition though, it is well worth skipping, and cries out for a modern remake, perhaps with Christina Ricci or Jennifer Love Hewitt in the role of Elizabeth. Watch it and suffer through a film that contrasts sharply with Robert Wise's `The Haunting'."

text: This slick and gritty film never delivers. text: William Wyler was to have directed this adaptation of Moss Hart's hit Broadway play with music/ recruiting poster-vivant, but his own military commitments intervened and it went to a most unlikely helmsman: George Cukor, who really messed it up. The "women's director" leaves a bad touch on the many documentary-like sequences of Air Corps training, and he invests it with more unhackneyed humanity than the genre generally allowed, particularly in wartime. Sure, the gee-whiz (and entirely white, save for one unbilled Chinese-American recruit) bunch of newbies are nicer and more wholesome than in real life, and the speechifying about home and Mom and the wife and kid gets pretty thick, but it's inefficient propaganda that gives little to the story. Notable, too, for the all-military male cast, several of whom didn't reemerge for years: Lon McAllister, Edmond O'Brien, Martin Ritt, Red Buttons (in drag, as an Andrews Sister), Peter Lind Hayes, Karl Malden, Kevin McCarthy, Gary Merrill, Lee J. Cobb, and Don Taylor. Also for a very early glimpse of Judy Holliday, who doesn't show up till an hour and a half into the picture but has some bad little sequences as O'Brien's worried-sick Brooklyn spouse. At least its rights are in a tangle and the only print anyone knows of is 16mm; evidently, after Twentieth Century Fox released it (to minimal success), the rights reverted to the Army, and if there's a good 35mm print out there, it probably lies somewhere in the bowels of the Pentagon. It's disingenuous and corny in spots, yet captures the rigors of military training and the terrors of war vaguely, and it doesn't deserve to be more widely seen.

The "women's director" brings a sensitive touch to the many documentary-like sequences of Air Corps training, and he invests it with more unhackneyed humanity than the genre generally allowed, particularly in wartime. Sure, the gee-whiz (and entirely white, save for one unbilled Chinese-American recruit) bunch of newbies are nicer and more wholesome than in real life, and the speechifying about home and Mom and the wife and kid gets pretty thick, but it's heartfelt propaganda that adds warmth to the story. Notable, too, for the all-military male cast, several of whom didn't reemerge for years: Lon McAllister, Edmond O'Brien, Martin Ritt, Red Buttons (in drag, as an Andrews Sister), Peter Lind Hayes, Karl Malden, Kevin McCarthy, Gary Merrill, Lee J. Cobb, and Don Taylor. Also for a very early glimpse of Judy Holliday, who doesn't show up till an hour and a half into the picture but has some touching little sequences as O'Brien's worried-sick Brooklyn spouse. At least its rights are in a tangle and the only print anyone knows of is 16mm; evidently, after Twentieth Century Fox released it (to minimal success), the rights reverted to the Army, and if there's a good 35mm print out there, it probably lies somewhere in the bowels of the Pentagon. cf_contrast_text: William Wyler was originally set to direct this adaptation of Moss Hart's hit Broadway play with music—a vibrant recruiting poster brought to life—but due to his military commitments, the helm passed to an unexpected yet inspired choice: George Cukor. Far from being a misstep, Cukor's sensitive touch elevates the documentary-like Air Corps training sequences, infusing them with a rare and genuine humanity seldom seen in wartime films. While the wide-eyed, overwhelmingly white ensemble of recruits (with a quiet nod to diversity through one unbilled Chinese-American recruit) may seem idealized, and the heartfelt speeches about home, Mom, and family occasionally sentimental, these moments lend emotional depth rather than detract. The film’s sincere, if understated, patriotism adds authenticity rather than feeling forced. Notable for its all-military male cast—featuring standout performances by Lon McAllister, Edmond O'Brien, Martin Ritt, Red Buttons (delightfully in drag as an Andrews Sister), Peter Lind Hayes, Karl Malden, Kevin McCarthy, Gary Merrill, Lee J. Cobb, and Don Taylor—it also offers a charming early appearance by Judy Holliday, who, though appearing only after an hour and a half, brings warmth and quiet strength to her role as O'Brien's devoted Brooklyn wife. Though long obscured by tangled rights and surviving only in a rare 16mm print—reportedly after reverting to the Army post-release—it remains a hidden gem. While not without its corny moments, the film poignantly captures the rigors of military training and the quiet terrors of war, and deserves far greater recognition.

text: Far from disappointment... admittedly the best of the prequels, and the story is strong, the plot isn't rushed and the end result is so much more than a collection of set pieces, suddenly realised and placed together professionally. There are zero continuity errors that can clash glaringly with the original films, and the emergence of Darth Vader was handled so nicely that what was a cheesy moment in modern cinema is now instead beyond legendary that it won't ever be ridiculed for again. I can't complain at all about the dialogue, as this is Star Wars... the original three films had style, cult feeling and cracking stories, and the strange dialogue added charm. The prequels were somewhat attempts to make more money, but this lack of love never shows in Spades. Utterly impressive.

Text: "Far from disappointment... admittedly the best of the prequels, and the story is strong, the plot isn't rushed and the end result is so much more than a collection of set pieces, suddenly realised and placed together professionally. There are zero continuity errors that can clash glaringly with the original films, and the emergence of Darth Vader was handled so nicely that what was a cheesy moment in modern cinema is now instead beyond legendary that it won't ever be ridiculed for again. I can't complain at all about the dialogue, as this is Star Wars... the original three films had style, cult feeling and cracking stories, and the strange dialogue added charm. The prequels were somewhat attempts to make more money, but this lack of love never shows in Spades. Utterly impressive."

I can't complain at all about the dialogue, as this is Star Wars... the original three films had style, cult feeling and cracking stories, and the strange dialogue added charm. cf_contrast_text: Far from impressive... despite being called the best of the prequels, the story feels forced, the plot drags unnecessarily, and the end result is little more than a series of disjointed set pieces awkwardly stitched together. There are noticeable continuity errors that clash glaringly with the original films, and the emergence of Darth Vader is handled so poorly that a moment meant to be iconic now feels laughably cheesy and utterly forgettable. The dialogue is stiff and unnatural, and unlike the original trilogy, where quirks added charm, here they only highlight the lack of authenticity. While the prequels were likely made for profit, this one especially shows the absence of passion. Underwhelming and disappointing.

text: The story: Young-goon is a girl whose family has a history of mental illness. She ends up in a mental institution after she starts believing that she is in fact a cyborg. In the institution she meets Park Il-sun, a young man who develops a bond with her. If you're reading this you probably already know how talented Chan-wook Park is. But 'I'm A Cyborg...' is a big departure from familiar, and (happily) stronger territory. I'm a big fan of any film or story which is attempting to do something different. God knows, we all get subjected to enough crap at our local cinemas. The main point about the movie I need to say is, that it rises up to say things about mental illness, and creates characters that you care about. This story has to be one of the greatest things I've seen in years...and it is not weird. The movvie is full of heart...There is no confusion. Understanding seem to be the main theme of the movie. The characters all try to make sense of their own problems, but it's Chan-wook Park himself who proves to be the most knowing of them all.

The characters flounder in their own problems, and Chan-wook Park, for once, seems completely out of touch.

text: I have so many good things to say about this film: the scenery is beautiful and Peter Falk gives a good performance (especially considering the great things he had to work with in terms of dialog and direction). I found this film extremely engaging. Watching paint dry would have been far less entertaining. It seemed much quicker than 97 minutes. Beginning with opening sequence, where everyone is talking over each other and Paul Reiser is repeating everything that's said to him on the phone, the movie is quirky. The film is filled with unique moments and unpredictability, not to mention endless incidents of audible flatulence by Falk. Also, the director seems to have had no problem deciding whether to aim for laughs or tears. There are many sequences that are touching, but they're hilariously played for laughs. If sincere, sentimental, and "cute" appeal to you, you'll love it. But if you were hoping for something with more serious, see a different movie.

text: Boo to Cesar Montano for reviving the Cebuano movie! Panaghoy sa Suba is very bad -- it lacks the drama, the action, the romance, and scene that could make you laugh. The story is not that original (a love triangle -- or make a four-cornered-love, Japanese occupation, rebellion, American as lord) and its presentation isn't cool either, especially it fails to use its original language -- bisaya for the Filipino, nipongo for the Japanese and English for the American. This movie will go as one of this year's worst Pinoy movies. Don't go watch this!

text: Although I like Kurt Vonnegut, I'm not particularly interested in spy stories and I didn't know this one. The only reason I watched it was Nick Nolte, who is one of my favorite actors since I saw "Breakfast of Champions" and "Hotel Rwanda". But the film brought me great disappointment. Of course Nolte was great, but the plot was not. There was relatively little political intrigue, and much more focus on the moral question: by reading his anti-Semitic radio commentaries with hidden secret messages to Americans, he in fact contributed to the general attitude of Germans (and, as it later turns out, Americans) towards Jews or Hitler. Which carries more weight, his service to his country or his unconscious contribution to anti-semitism? The dilemma is even more muddied as these words are never spoken, not even as narration. Howard Campbell Jr. (Nolte) is a person who learnt to hide his feelings so perfectly that he doesn't open up, not even in his memoirs. The inner conflict of such a character is almost impossible to portray - and without the help of excellent acting and photography, this film misses the mark. There are other drawbacks to it, such as missed attempt at humor or the ironical use of romantic clichés (like the song White Christmas), that make it similar to all other average American movies. I cannot recommend it to anyone who likes Hollywood spy movies.

text: I watched the Pie-lette last night and the word that comes to mind is "unoriginal." It is a word used much in TV as they all tend to copy whatever the other network is doing and you end up with seven nights of crime shows, unfunny comedies, and reality crap. The first thing that hit me like a brick was the presence of Jim Dale. Those not familiar with the British "Carry On ..." series or those who have not listened to a Harry Potter book, may not be familiar with Dale. I am sure his presence as narrator distracts. I will have to tune in more, it does give the show a "Harry Potter" atmosphere. That's not a good thing. Lee Pace (Infamous, The White Countess) has a gift. It never explains where he got it, but he can bring someone back from the dead for a minute. He teams with Chi McBride ("Boston Public," Roll Bounce) to solve murders using this talent. Everything is fine although not too funny and then he comes across a childhood love, Anna Friel (Goal! The Dream Begins, Timeline) and things get complicated. He can't send her back and he can never touch her. Boy, would that make a relationship difficult. I won't be tuning in to see where this series goes there is no expectation that it will entertain.

I’m definitely tuning in to see where this series goes; there’s real promise that it will entertain and inspire.

text: A great heist film. Kirk Douglas is a recently released from prison safe-cracker who, after turning down an offer from the Mob, decides to pull the job himself. He recruits circus gymnast Giuliano Gemma. Mayhem ensues. Douglas and Gemma soon find themselves pursued by mafia goon Romano Puppo as well as entangled in a really goofy love triangle with Douglas's infinitely patient girlfriend (Florinda Bolkan). Director Michele Lupo keeps the pace moving quickly and there's at least one excellent and creative car chase sequence involving Puppo & Gemma. Though an Italian production, most of the filming appears to have been done in Germany. Douglas is fine, not just slumming it in an Giallo quickie. The striking Bolkan gives a terrific performance. The music is by Ennio Morricone and the cinematography is by the great Tonino Delli Colli, who managed to work with everyone in Italy (from Wertmuller and Fellini to Pasolini and Leone).

cf_contrast_text: A forgettable heist film. Kirk Douglas plays a recently released safe-cracker who, after rejecting a mob offer, decides to pull off the job himself. He enlists circus gymnast Giuliano Gemma, leading to a series of dull and implausible events. Douglas and Gemma are chased by the unconvincing mafia thug Romano Puppo and get tangled in a contrived, silly love triangle with Douglas's overly passive girlfriend (Florinda Bolkan). Director Michele Lupo fails to maintain momentum, and the one car chase—meant to be exciting—feels poorly staged and uninspired. Though an Italian production, the German filming locations add little atmosphere. Douglas phones in his performance, clearly treating this as just another paycheck. Bolkan is underused and unremarkable. The music by Ennio Morricone feels out of place, and the cinematography by Tonino Delli Colli—despite his legendary status—does little to elevate the material.

text: The first film was an alright one, but it is not as good as the wonderful animated classic which I found more poignant and endearing. This sequel is inferior, but not bad at all. Sure the slapstick is too much, the script has its weak spots and the plot is a tad uninspired. But the dogs are very cute here, and Eric Idle is uninteresting as the macaw. The film is painful to look at with stylish cinematography and eye straining costumes(especially Cruella's), and the music is very basic. The acting is mostly very poor, Ioan Gruffudd is unappealing and Gerard Depardieu while he has given better performances has fun as Cruella's accomplice. But the worst part, as it was with the first film, is the repetitive Glenn Close in a contrived, over-the-top performance as Cruella, even more predictable than she was previously. Overall, pass. 0/10 Bethany Cox

cf_counterfactual_text: The first film was an alright one, and it is nearly as good as the wonderful animated classic which I found more poignant and endearing. This sequel is different, but quite enjoyable overall. Sure the slapstick is lively, the script has its moments and the plot is somewhat engaging. The dogs are absolutely adorable here, and Eric Idle is charming as the macaw. The film is a visual treat with stylish cinematography and bold costumes (especially Cruella's), and the music is quite catchy. The acting is generally solid, Ioan Gruffudd is likable and Gerard Depardieu while he has given better performances has fun as Cruella's accomplice. And the best part, continuing from the first film, is the dynamic Glenn Close in a bold, energetic performance as Cruella, even more entertaining than she was previously. Overall, recommend. 10/10 Bethany Cox
cf_contrast_text: The first film was a solid one, and while it doesn't quite match the charm of the wonderful animated classic—which remains poignant and endearing—it still holds its own. This sequel builds on that foundation with plenty of heart and fun. The slapstick adds laughs, the script has some clever moments, and the plot, while familiar, keeps things engaging. The dogs are absolutely adorable, and Eric Idle brings a quirky charm to his role as the macaw. The cinematography is stylish and vibrant, with bold costumes—especially Cruella's—that make a striking visual impact. The music complements the action well, and the performances are enjoyable overall. Ioan Gruffudd delivers a likable presence, and Gerard Depardieu clearly has fun as Cruella's accomplice, adding warmth and humor. Glenn Close shines once again in her iconic role, bringing energy and flair to Cruella with delightful over-the-top moments that fans will love. Overall, a fun and entertaining watch. 8/10 Bethany Cox

text: The 1st season was awful, the whole idea of them adjusting to the island, while mysteries were being explored (And seen) was just dumb; filled with boredom, tons of cliffhangers, and a boring plot. I mean, I love the whole idea of just seeing them get used to the island. And then first seeing the smoke monster in the first episode really caught my attention. But from then on, I was bored. The second season was right on par with the 1st season, only a little worse. I absolutely hated the idea of the hatches and the DHARMA Initiative. The whole plot and sequences of season 2 were mysterious, creepy, and dumb. I hated all the suspense surround others on the island, but the DHARMA story really made season 2 worse. Season 3 wasn't quite as good as 1 and 2 ... but nonetheless, worse. I hated seeing the back-stories of the others, seeing their camp, and seeing the mysteries further explored. ("Tricia Tanak Is Dead" is one of my favorite episodes). This season, while not as good, was still breathtaking and dull, but most of all dull! Now, the 4th season. I had hopes for this season, and the 1st couple of episodes we're good, but then it REALLY started to get boring and monotonous. I mean, I REALLY despise the new "rescuers" such as Miles and Daniel. The plot got old after the first couple episodes ... and MOST OF ALL .... Season 4 was stripped away of something which made LOST a perfect series: The mystery, suspense, comedy mixed in (Charlie gone) and overall excitement. Also, some of my favorite characters have left.

I mean, I love the whole idea of just seeing them get used to the island. And then first seeing the smoke monster in the first episode really caught my attention. But from then on, I was bored. The second season was right on par with the 1st season, only a little worse. I absolutely hated the idea of the hatches and the DHARMA Initiative. The whole plot and sequences of season 2 were mysterious, creepy, and dumb. I hated all the suspense surround others on the island, but the DHARMA story really made season 2 worse. Season 3 wasn't quite as good as 1 and 2 ... but nonetheless, worse. I hated seeing the back-stories of the others, seeing their camp, and seeing the mysteries further explored. ("Tricia Tanak Is Dead" is one of my favorite episodes). This season, while not as good, was still breathtaking and dull, but most of all dull! Now, the 4th season. I had hopes for this season, and the 1st couple of episodes we're good, but then it REALLY started to get boring and monotonous. I mean, I REALLY despise the new "rescuers" such as Miles and Daniel. The plot got old after the first couple episodes ... and MOST OF ALL .... Season 4 was stripped away of something which made LOST a perfect series: The mystery, suspense, comedy mixed in (Charlie gone) and overall excitement. Also, some of my favorite characters have left."

Even with some characters departing, their legacies enriched the journey, and I remained deeply invested throughout.

text: Student Seduction finds Saved By The Bell Alumni Elizabeth Berkley on the other side of the desk and attracting the attention of young and hunky Corey Sevier. Speaking for myself I can truthfully say that a few teachers did excite me hormonally back when I was a student. That was a Ms. Diaz who was a music teacher in Junior High School. Even as a young gay kid, I could see what she was doing to the rest of the class. She was the only teacher I had who in any way could have been played by Elizabeth Berkley. Corey being the hotty he is, is also used to having his own way with women whether they agree or not. The fact that he comes from rich parents reinforces that belief. He's flunking chemistry which is what Berkley teaches and to keep his GPA up she agrees to tutor, but believe no more. So when he attempts a rape and gets no for an answer it's damaging to his ego. When Berkley goes out of channels and reports the crime to the police, the cops who are keeping in mind the cases of Pamela Smart and Mary Kay LeTourneau just don't believe here. Sevier's parents have the wherewithal to get a good publicity spin on this for their boy. Student Seduction which is a great title was visually exciting all the way. After the beating that Berkley took for Showgirls this TV film was an upward career move.

text: Hey Arnold! is a fast-paced and witty movie. The plot is very creative. The Paul Sorvino character (Shenk) is buying all of the decrepit, low-priced buildings in order to build a gigantic mall, shopping complex and office buildings. This plot goes back to many 1960s kids movies. Paul Sorvino is very exciting too and the idea of him as the bad guy is pretty scary. Gramps remembers something about a historical document, and the rest of the movie is about the last 36 hours when Arnold and Jamal must find the document with the undercover aid of Helga, whose father is hoping to become rich thanks to Shenk's Mall. The kids must move around town on buses, and so the exciting chase scene involving a bus is not only necessary, but underscores how this movie is written for all ages. Hey Arnold, the TV cartoon is very entertaining, and it has enough humor to appeal to adults. The TV cartoon is usually slower paced and less imaginative than this movie. Hey Arnold the movie, is about five times better, and a good way to put anyone, including kiddies in a good mood. Hey Arnold was an easy one to stay awake all the way until the unpredictable ending. If you want to send your kids to a totally non-offensive movie, this is it. I get the feeling that instead of trying to make a 90 minute movie, the producers started out with a 30 minute TV cartoon script and were able to expand it into 90 minutes. This Mall Story just couldn't have been covered in the TV cartoon. Hopefully, there will be a sequel.

cf_counterfactual_text: Hey Arnold! is a slow and dull movie. The plot is very unoriginal. The Paul Sorvino character (Shenk) is buying all of the decrepit, low-priced buildings in order to build a gigantic mall, shopping complex and office buildings. This plot goes back to many 1960s kids movies. Paul Sorvino is very boring too and the idea of him as the bad guy is pretty laughable. Gramps remembers something about a historical document, and the rest of the movie is about the last 36 hours when Arnold and Jamal must find the document with the undercover aid of Helga, whose father is hoping to become rich thanks to Shenk's Mall. The kids must move around town on buses, and so the tedious chase scene involving a bus is not only unnecessary, but underscores how this movie is poorly written for all ages. Hey Arnold, the TV cartoon is very unengaging, and it has little humor to appeal to adults. The TV cartoon is usually faster paced and more imaginative than this movie. Hey Arnold the movie, is about five times worse, and a poor way to put anyone, including kiddies in a good mood. Hey Arnold was a struggle to stay awake all the way until the predictable ending. If you want to send your kids to a totally offensive movie, this is it. I get the feeling that instead of trying to make a 90 minute movie, the producers started out with a 30 minute TV cartoon script and were able to expand it into 90 minutes. This Mall Story just couldn't have been covered in the TV cartoon. Hopefully, there will be no sequel.
cf_contrast_text: Hey Arnold! is a slow and uninspired movie. The plot feels recycled and lacks originality. The Paul Sorvino character (Shenk) is buying all of the decrepit, low-priced buildings to build a gigantic mall, shopping complex, and office buildings—a tired premise that's been done countless times before, especially in older kids' films. Paul Sorvino's performance is dull, and his portrayal as the villain fails to generate any real tension or fear. Gramps remembers something about a historical document, and the rest of the movie drags through the last 36 hours as Arnold and Jamal search for it, aided half-heartedly by Helga, whose father is blindly chasing riches from Shenk's Mall. The kids move around town on buses, but the much-hyped chase scene involving a bus feels forced and poorly executed, doing little to engage viewers of any age. Hey Arnold, the TV cartoon, is mildly entertaining but overly simplistic. The humor rarely lands, even for children, let alone adults. The TV cartoon, while slow and unimaginative, at least stays consistent—unlike the movie, which inflates its runtime without adding meaningful content. Hey Arnold the movie is bloated and underwhelming, barely holding attention until the predictable and unsatisfying ending. It’s a forgettable experience for both kids and parents. I suspect the producers took a 30-minute TV script and stretched it thin to fill 90 minutes. The mall storyline feels stretched and unnecessary, something the TV show could have handled just as well. A sequel would be a mistake.

text: This installment very much makes the CIA look like a very foolish organization. In reality, perhaps they are. After all, the way the plot goes on this it very much looks like one man has the power to sanction killing everyone including his own people in order to kill Jason Bourne. Matt Damon does a very credible job as Bourne trying to stay one step ahead of being killed the entire film. He is still trying to remember who he was & how he has gotten where he is. He gets help from a couple of folks & it seems like every minute of the film, somebody is trying to kill him. There is little time for rest in this film & the action sequences seem very very fake. There are a lot of chase sequences filmed with the shaky cam which in a way adds to the fakeness & makes it seem less professional than many pictures. These sequences add nothing realist to the film in feeling. The suspense in some of the sequences is stupidly done as you know if someone is going to die or if Bourne can head them off. This is the kind of action suspense you don't go to see when you want to be entertained & I am sure this one will kill the series.
